summary: The McLain Family Band is really unique because we play bluegrass music deeply rooted in our region, but also enriched by a wide variety of influences. Our father, with his background in music theory from Denison University and Harvard, and folklore studies at UNC Chapel Hill, brought a broad musical perspective that shaped our sound. We grew up in Hindman, Kentucky, and played music that connected us to our community and to the traditions of the mountains. Bill Monroe was a close friend who appreciated our creativity and originality, which blended traditional bluegrass with other musical elements.
cues: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

summary: Our father studied music theory at Denison University, which was a foundational part of his musical education. This background helped him understand composition deeply and influenced the way he approached music with us. Denison was the starting point before he went on to graduate work at Harvard and later folklore studies at UNC Chapel Hill. His academic training there contributed to the unique blend of traditional and innovative music we played in the McLain Family Band.

After Denison, our father pursued graduate work in composition at Harvard University. There, one of his instructors critiqued his work for sounding too much like folk music, which he found amusing and embraced. Harvard was an important step in his formal music education, but it was clear that folk and traditional music were central to his identity. This blend of academic rigor and folk roots shaped the music he shared with us and ultimately the McLain Family Band's style.

entity: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
summary: Our father also studied folklore at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, which added another dimension to his understanding of music and culture. This graduate work in folklore connected him to the traditions and stories behind the music we played. It was part of why he valued the regional mountain music so much and encouraged us to play it authentically. UNC Chapel Hill helped him bridge academic study with the living traditions of Kentucky where we grew up.

The Hindman Settlement School in Hindman, Kentucky, was central to our family's life. Our mother worked there as a recreation director, and our father served as recreation director and later executive director. Growing up there immersed us in the local culture and mountain music, which deeply influenced the McLain Family Band's repertoire. The school was more than a workplace; it was a community hub that connected us to the traditions we honored in our music.

Hindman, Kentucky, is where we grew up and where the roots of our music run deep. It’s a place rich in mountain music traditions, and our family was very involved in the community through the Hindman Settlement School. Living there allowed us to be surrounded by the sounds and culture that shaped the McLain Family Band. It was the backdrop for our musical upbringing and the place where our father brought his diverse musical influences together with local traditions.

Bill Monroe was not just a legendary figure in bluegrass; he was a dear friend to us. We had many personal conversations with him during festivals in the early 1970s, often walking together away from the crowds. He appreciated our creativity and originality, and he loved that we incorporated diverse influences into our music. He even played with us on occasion and was supportive of our band's innovations, despite the common perception that he boxed bluegrass music in a strict way.

Our father’s record collection included The Beatles, which was part of the broad musical landscape we grew up with. Even though we played bluegrass, having exposure to pop groups like The Beatles expanded our musical horizons. This diverse listening helped shape the unique sound of the McLain Family Band by blending traditional and contemporary influences. The Beatles were just one example of the many styles that influenced our father and, through him, us.

Three Dog Night was another band included in our father’s wide-ranging record collection. Their music, alongside others like The Beatles and classical composers, contributed to the eclectic musical environment we were raised in. This variety helped us appreciate different styles and incorporate elements beyond traditional bluegrass into our own music. It was part of what made the McLain Family Band’s sound original and broad.

Our father played recorder and even performed pieces like a Brandenburg concerto movement by Johann Sebastian Bach with us. Bach’s music was part of the classical influences that blended into our upbringing alongside folk and bluegrass. This classical element added a layer of complexity and richness to the McLain Family Band’s music. It showed how our musical education was not limited to one genre but embraced many traditions.

Beethoven was among the classical composers whose music our father introduced to us through his record collection. This exposure to Beethoven’s work was part of the broad musical foundation that influenced the McLain Family Band. It helped us appreciate the depth and range of music beyond bluegrass, enriching our understanding and performance. Beethoven’s presence in our musical life reflected our father’s respect for all kinds of music.

Scott Joplin’s ragtime piano pieces were part of the musical influences our father shared with us. He himself played ragtime piano, which connected us to that lively and rhythmic style. Joplin’s music was one of many genres that contributed to the McLain Family Band’s unique sound, blending traditional mountain music with other American musical forms. This diversity was a hallmark of our family’s approach to music.

Burl Ives was included in our father’s extensive record collection, representing folk music traditions. His music was part of the cultural tapestry that influenced the McLain Family Band. Through Burl Ives and others, we connected to folk songs from various cultures, which enriched our understanding of music’s role in community and storytelling. This helped us appreciate the broader folk tradition beyond just bluegrass.

Jackson, Kentucky, was the site of a festival in the early 1970s where we had a television show on the local Hazard TV station. Bill Monroe was a guest on that show, which was a memorable moment for us. The festival and the TV appearance were part of the McLain Family Band’s active engagement with the Kentucky music scene. Jackson was one of the places where our music connected with a wider audience and where we shared the stage with legends like Bill Monroe.

Hazard, Kentucky, was where we filmed a television show in the early 1970s that helped promote the McLain Family Band. The studio was small and modest, but it was an important platform for us to share our music. Bill Monroe even came to be a guest on the show, which was a highlight. Hazard was part of the regional music circuit that supported bluegrass and mountain music during that time.

The Osborne Brothers were influential in bringing electric instruments like electric bass and drums into bluegrass music, which was something Bill Monroe was more cautious about. They did a great job incorporating these elements while respecting the tradition. Their approach showed how bluegrass music evolved over time, and they were part of the changing festival scenes we experienced. The Osborne Brothers’ innovations complemented the creative spirit that Bill Monroe supported.

Arnold Schultz was one of the influential musicians connected to Bill Monroe’s musical heritage, particularly known for his guitar and blues influence. His contributions were part of the rich mix of styles that shaped bluegrass music. We respected these roots deeply as we grew up playing in the McLain Family Band. Schultz’s legacy reminded us of the diverse cultural threads woven into the music we loved.
